subtopic_detail: NCSP - age to commence and interval for routine cervical screening
stem: A 24-year-old woman attends her GP for a routine check. She has never had a cervical screening test. She became sexually active at age 18, has had three lifetime partners, currently uses condoms with a regular partner, and has had no abnormal bleeding, postcoital bleeding, intermenstrual bleeding or discharge. She received the routine school-based HPV vaccination (Gardasil) in year 7 at age 12 and completed her full course. She has never been pregnant, has no family history of gynaecological cancer, and is otherwise well. Her cycles are regular at 28 days with normal flow. She is not on any contraception other than condoms. She asks about cervical screening because a friend at work recently had a test.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 70 bpm, T 36.5 degC.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, non-tender. No palpable masses. Speculum not performed at this visit.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice regarding cervical screening for this patient?

options.text: Commence routine 5-yearly HPV cervical screening tests starting at age 25
options.rationale: Under the renewed National Cervical Screening Program (NCSP), the primary screening test is HPV-based, performed every 5 years from age 25 to 74 in people with a cervix, regardless of HPV vaccination status. This patient should be invited to start screening at age 25 (NCSP National Cancer Screening Register; Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Perform a cervical screening test today as she is sexually active
options.rationale: The NCSP entry age is 25; earlier screening is not recommended because HPV infection is common but transient in young people, and earlier screening increases harms (over-investigation) without reducing cervical cancer mortality (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Perform a co-test of HPV plus liquid-based cytology at today's visit
options.rationale: Co-testing is reserved for specific indications (test of cure after treated HSIL, symptomatic patients, DES exposure), not for routine primary screening, and is not indicated below the screening start age (NCSP National Cancer Screening Register).

options.text: No screening is required as she has completed HPV vaccination
options.rationale: Gardasil and Gardasil 9 do not cover every oncogenic HPV type, and vaccinated people still require routine screening from age 25 every 5 years (NCSP National Cancer Screening Register; NCIRS HPV vaccination).

options.text: Begin annual liquid-based cytology from age 25 with HPV only if abnormal
options.rationale: Australia transitioned from 2-yearly cytology to 5-yearly primary HPV testing in December 2017; LBC is now used as reflex triage on the same sample, not as the primary screen (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

explanation.summary: The Australian NCSP screens every 5 years from age 25 to 74 with a primary HPV test, regardless of HPV vaccination. Reflex liquid-based cytology occurs automatically on the same sample if a non-16/18 oncogenic HPV type is detected (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
key_points: Entry age 25, exit age 74 with an exit test between 70 and 74
Interval 5 years if HPV not detected on a satisfactory sample
HPV vaccination does not change screening eligibility or interval
Self-collection eligibility expanded to all eligible screeners from July 2022
Co-test (HPV + LBC) only for specific indications (test of cure, symptomatic patients, DES exposure)
explanation.pearls: If a patient asks why screening starts at 25 when sexual debut is younger - the rationale is that earlier screening increases harms (colposcopy, over-treatment of transient HPV) without reducing cancer mortality (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

tags: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health
stem: A 42-year-old Aboriginal woman attends an Aboriginal Community Controlled Health Service for a general check. Her last cervical screening test was a Pap smear 9 years ago, which she recalls as normal. She declines a clinician-collected speculum examination today, citing a history of childhood sexual abuse and significant discomfort with previous examinations. She has had three lifetime partners, is currently in a monogamous relationship, has had no postcoital or intermenstrual bleeding, no abnormal discharge, and no pelvic pain. She has never had genital warts or a diagnosis of high-grade dysplasia and has no immunosuppression. She received Gardasil as an adolescent through a catch-up program. She is parous (G3P3, all vaginal deliveries), her cycles are regular, BMI 27, and she is otherwise well. She is keen to participate in screening in a way she can tolerate. The clinic is accredited to offer self-collection.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no palpable mass. External genital examination declined.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in cervical screening?

options.text: Refer to gynaecology for examination under anaesthesia and cervical sampling
options.rationale: EUA is reserved for women in whom screening cannot be performed even with self-collection and who are at high pre-test risk; it is disproportionate first-line care in an asymptomatic patient (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Offer a self-collected vaginal HPV swab today as her routine cervical screening test
options.rationale: Since July 2022, all people eligible for cervical screening can choose self-collection, performed by the patient using a flocked swab (vaginal sample), with equivalent sensitivity for oncogenic HPV detection. This is particularly important for under-screened groups including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander women and survivors of sexual violence (NCSP National Cancer Screening Register; Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Decline screening today and rebook for clinician-collected sample at a later visit
options.rationale: She is 9 years overdue and a self-collection option exists that she can tolerate; deferring screening risks continued non-participation and is contrary to the purpose of the 2022 self-collection expansion (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Send a self-collected urine sample for HPV testing in lieu of vaginal sampling
options.rationale: Urine HPV testing is not part of the Australian NCSP pathway; the approved self-collected sample is a vaginal flocked swab (NCSP National Cancer Screening Register).

options.text: Reassure her that prior Gardasil vaccination removes the need for screening
options.rationale: HPV vaccination does not eliminate the requirement for routine cervical screening as not all oncogenic types are covered; she should be screened to NCSP standards (NCIRS HPV vaccination).

explanation.summary: Self-collection (vaginal flocked swab for HPV) became universally available within the NCSP in July 2022 and is particularly important for under-screened groups. It has equivalent sensitivity to clinician-collected sampling for oncogenic HPV detection (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
key_points: Universal self-collection eligibility since 1 July 2022
Approved sample is a vaginal flocked swab (not urine)
Equivalent sensitivity to clinician-collected HPV testing
Reflex LBC requires clinician-collected sample - patient is recalled if HPV non-16/18 detected on self-collected sample
Patients screened directly by colposcopy if HPV 16/18 is detected on a self-collected sample
explanation.pearls: If a self-collected sample detects non-16/18 oncogenic HPV, the patient must return for a clinician-collected sample for reflex LBC. If HPV 16/18 is detected, the patient is referred directly to colposcopy (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

subtopic_detail: NCSP - HPV 16/18 positive result direct colposcopy pathway
stem: A 31-year-old G1P1 woman receives the result of her first cervical screening test, taken 2 weeks ago. The clinician-collected sample is reported as: HPV oncogenic 16 detected. Reflex liquid-based cytology shows possible low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (pLSIL). She is asymptomatic with no postcoital bleeding, no intermenstrual bleeding, and no abnormal discharge. She had a normal vaginal delivery 18 months ago, breastfed for 12 months, and is currently using the etonogestrel implant. She is in a monogamous relationship of 4 years. She received Gardasil as a teenager but is unsure if she completed the full course. She is otherwise well with no immunosuppression, no organ transplant, and is not on corticosteroids. She has no family history of cervical or other gynaecological cancer. She is anxious because she has read online about HPV 16 and cancer and asks what should happen next.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft. External genitalia normal. Speculum at the time of sample: cervix appeared normal with no visible lesions, no contact bleeding.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat HPV cervical screening test in 12 months to assess for clearance
options.rationale: A 12-month repeat is the pathway for non-16/18 oncogenic HPV with negative or pLSIL reflex cytology; with HPV 16 detected, direct referral for colposcopy is required regardless of cytology result (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Repeat the HPV test using a self-collected sample to confirm HPV 16
options.rationale: There is no role for confirmatory re-testing of a positive 16/18 result; the laboratory has performed a validated assay and the next step is colposcopy (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Administer Gardasil 9 catch-up vaccination now to clear the established HPV 16 infection
options.rationale: HPV vaccines are prophylactic, not therapeutic; they do not clear established infection. Catch-up vaccination may still be offered separately but does not alter colposcopy referral (NCIRS HPV vaccination).

options.text: Treat empirically with cervical cryotherapy in primary care without histological confirmation
options.rationale: Empirical ablative treatment without colposcopic and histological confirmation is contrary to a see-and-treat threshold and risks under- or over-treatment; histology must guide management (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Refer directly to colposcopy now regardless of the reflex cytology result
options.rationale: Under the NCSP, detection of HPV 16 or 18 on a screening sample mandates direct referral to colposcopy regardless of reflex LBC result, because of the substantially higher risk of underlying CIN3+ associated with these genotypes (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines; NCSP National Cancer Screening Register).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

explanation.summary: Under the NCSP, HPV 16 or 18 detection triggers direct colposcopy referral regardless of reflex LBC findings due to the high risk of underlying high-grade dysplasia. Non-16/18 oncogenic HPV is triaged by reflex LBC (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
key_points: HPV 16/18 positive: direct colposcopy regardless of LBC
Non-16/18 oncogenic HPV + negative/pLSIL LBC: repeat HPV in 12 months
Non-16/18 oncogenic HPV + HSIL or worse LBC: colposcopy
Unsatisfactory sample: repeat within 6-12 weeks
Symptomatic patients (e.g. postcoital bleeding) require co-test and colposcopy regardless of result
explanation.pearls: HPV vaccination is prophylactic - it does not clear established infection. Discuss this when patients ask if catch-up vaccination will 'fix' a positive screen (NCIRS HPV vaccination).

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

subtopic_detail: NCSP - non-16/18 oncogenic HPV with pLSIL reflex cytology
stem: A 38-year-old G2P2 woman returns to her GP for her routine cervical screening test result. The clinician-collected sample is reported as: HPV oncogenic non-16/18 detected; reflex liquid-based cytology shows possible low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (pLSIL). She is asymptomatic with no postcoital bleeding, no intermenstrual bleeding and no abnormal discharge. She is in a monogamous relationship of 12 years and was last screened (Pap smear) 7 years ago, reported as normal at the time. She is a non-smoker, BMI 24, has no immunosuppression, no organ transplant and is not on corticosteroids. She received Gardasil as a teenager. She is anxious because she has read online about possible cancer and asks specifically about colposcopy. Her two pregnancies were uncomplicated vaginal deliveries. She has no family history of cervical, endometrial, ovarian or breast cancer. She uses the levonorgestrel intrauterine system for contraception and her periods are minimal.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no palpable mass. Cervix appeared normal at speculum at the time of sample with no visible lesion and no contact bleeding.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer to colposcopy now because reflex cytology shows possible LSIL on this sample
options.rationale: Non-16/18 oncogenic HPV with negative LBC, pLSIL or LSIL is at intermediate risk and is managed by repeat HPV testing at 12 months in immunocompetent patients, not direct colposcopy (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Repeat clinician-collected HPV cervical screening test in 12 months from today
options.rationale: For non-16/18 oncogenic HPV detected with reflex LBC showing negative, pLSIL or LSIL, the recommended NCSP pathway is repeat HPV testing at 12 months. If oncogenic HPV is again detected, the patient is referred to colposcopy; if HPV is not detected, she returns to routine 5-yearly screening (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines; NCSP National Cancer Screening Register).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Repeat HPV cervical screening test in 6 months as an interim safety check
options.rationale: A 6-month interval is not part of the NCSP pathway for intermediate-risk non-16/18 HPV; the recommended interval is 12 months (NCSP National Cancer Screening Register).

options.text: Return to routine 5-yearly screening as cytology is only low-grade
options.rationale: Returning to 5-yearly screening would miss progression in the cohort with persistent oncogenic HPV; the patient must be re-tested at 12 months to demonstrate clearance (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Refer to colposcopy at 6 months if symptoms develop and routine screening otherwise
options.rationale: A symptom-triggered approach abandons surveillance for persistent HPV and is not the NCSP pathway; structured 12-month repeat HPV testing is required (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

explanation.summary: Non-16/18 oncogenic HPV with negative/pLSIL/LSIL reflex cytology is intermediate-risk and managed by repeat HPV at 12 months. Most transient infections clear in this interval; persistent infection or progression to HSIL on cytology prompts colposcopy (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
key_points: Intermediate risk pathway: non-16/18 HPV + negative/pLSIL/LSIL LBC -> 12-month repeat HPV
Higher risk: non-16/18 HPV + possible HSIL/HSIL/glandular abnormality -> colposcopy
Highest risk: any HPV 16/18 -> direct colposcopy regardless of LBC
Immunosuppressed patients have a different pathway with closer surveillance
Symptomatic patients always co-tested and referred regardless of result
explanation.pearls: The 12-month repeat is HPV-based, not cytology-based. Many transient HPV infections clear in this interval; only persistent infection requires colposcopy (NCSP National Cancer Screening Register).

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

subtopic_detail: HPV vaccination - Australian schedule (Gardasil 9, single-dose schedule from 2023)
stem: A 14-year-old female adolescent attends a GP clinic with her mother for a general check after recently arriving in Australia from overseas. She has not received any HPV vaccination previously. She has no allergies, takes no regular medications, and is otherwise well, with menarche at age 12 and regular cycles. She has never been sexually active. Routine childhood vaccinations were completed in her country of origin but did not include HPV. She is up to date with school enrolment. She has no immunosuppression, no organ transplant, and is not on long-term corticosteroids. Her mother specifically asks about the HPV vaccine schedule because she has read conflicting information online about whether one or two doses are required. The GP confirms the patient is generally well, has had no recent febrile illness, and is up to date with all other Australian National Immunisation Program vaccines. She is keen to receive the vaccine today if it is offered for free.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, T 36.5 degC.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate HPV vaccination plan for this patient under the National Immunisation Program?

options.text: Three doses of Gardasil 9 at 0, 2 and 6 months because she is starting late
options.rationale: A three-dose schedule is reserved for immunocompromised individuals; immunocompetent people up to age 25 receive a single dose under the 2023 schedule (NCIRS HPV vaccination; Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Two doses of Gardasil 9 at 0 and 6 months because she is over 14
options.rationale: Two-dose schedules were used prior to February 2023; the current Australian schedule is a single dose for immunocompetent people aged 9 to 25 (NCIRS HPV vaccination).

options.text: Single dose of Gardasil 9 today, funded under the National Immunisation Program
options.rationale: Since February 2023 the Australian NIP HPV schedule is a single dose of Gardasil 9 for immunocompetent people aged 9 to 25, including catch-up. Routine school-based delivery is in year 7 (around age 12-13) but free catch-up is funded up to and including age 25 (NCIRS HPV vaccination; Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Defer HPV vaccination until age 18 when sexual debut is more likely
options.rationale: HPV vaccination is most effective before HPV exposure; deferring is contrary to the rationale of the program and removes protection during the highest-risk period (NCIRS HPV vaccination).

options.text: Single dose of bivalent Cervarix today as she is over 14 years old
options.rationale: Cervarix (bivalent HPV 16/18) is not the funded NIP vaccine in Australia; Gardasil 9 (9-valent) is the program vaccine and provides broader genotype coverage (NCIRS HPV vaccination).

explanation.summary: Since February 2023, the Australian NIP HPV schedule has been a single dose of Gardasil 9 for immunocompetent people aged 9 to 25 years, including catch-up. Three doses remain recommended for the severely immunocompromised. School delivery is in year 7 (NCIRS HPV vaccination; Australian Immunisation Handbook).
key_points: Single dose Gardasil 9 for immunocompetent people aged 9-25
Three-dose schedule (0, 2, 6 months) for severe immunocompromise
Routine school-based program delivers in year 7 (ages 12-13)
Catch-up free under NIP up to and including age 25
Vaccination does not change cervical screening requirements
explanation.pearls: Australia was the first country to move to a single-dose HPV schedule on the National Immunisation Program (NCIRS HPV vaccination).

subtopic_detail: Symptomatic cervical cancer - postcoital bleeding workup overrides routine screening
stem: A 35-year-old G1P1 woman attends her GP with a 4-month history of intermittent postcoital bleeding occurring after most episodes of intercourse, with one episode of intermenstrual bleeding lasting 2 days. She has had no abnormal discharge, no dyspareunia and no pelvic pain. Her last cervical screening test was 18 months ago and showed HPV not detected on a clinician-collected sample. She received Gardasil as a teenager and is otherwise well. She is a current smoker (15 pack-years) and uses the combined oral contraceptive pill. She has one child after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery. There is no significant family history. She has been treated empirically by another clinician for presumed chlamydial cervicitis with azithromycin 1 g stat 6 weeks ago, with chlamydia and gonorrhoea NAAT both negative; symptoms have persisted unchanged. BMI is 24. She has no fevers, no weight loss, no urinary symptoms and no bowel symptoms. She is asking for a 'repeat HPV swab to make sure'.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 80 bpm, T 36.6 degC.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no masses. Speculum: cervix has a friable area at 4 o'clock with contact bleeding on swab; no obvious exophytic lesion. Bimanual: uterus normal size, no adnexal mass, no cervical motion tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative. Chlamydia/gonorrhoea NAAT negative 6 weeks ago. HPV screening test 18 months ago: HPV not detected.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat the routine NCSP HPV cervical screening test and act on the result
options.rationale: Routine HPV screening is for asymptomatic women; symptomatic patients with persistent postcoital bleeding require a co-test PLUS direct referral for colposcopy regardless of the HPV result, because a negative HPV does not exclude all cervical pathology in a symptomatic patient (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Empirical treatment for cervicitis with doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are not appropriate when STI testing is negative and symptoms have already failed empirical therapy; persistent postcoital bleeding with a friable cervix requires structural assessment (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Reassure patient that symptoms will likely resolve and review in 6 months
options.rationale: Watchful waiting in a smoker with 4 months of postcoital bleeding and a visibly friable cervix risks missing cervical cancer; this is an unsafe disposition (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Perform a co-test (HPV + LBC) and refer for colposcopy regardless of result
options.rationale: Symptomatic cervical bleeding (postcoital, intermenstrual or postmenopausal) is an indication for a co-test PLUS direct referral to colposcopy regardless of HPV or LBC results, because cytology can be falsely negative in invasive cancer and a recent negative HPV does not exclude pathology. A visibly abnormal cervix in a symptomatic patient is colposcopy-urgent (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Discontinue the combined oral contraceptive pill as likely cause, review 3 months
options.rationale: Breakthrough bleeding on the COCP rarely presents with reproducible postcoital bleeding and a friable cervix; structural pathology must be excluded first (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Persistent postcoital bleeding, particularly with a visibly abnormal cervix, mandates a co-test PLUS colposcopy regardless of the HPV result, because cervical cancer can present with cytology- and HPV-negative findings. A recent negative HPV screen does not exclude invasive disease in a symptomatic patient (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
key_points: Symptomatic cervical bleeding: co-test AND colposcopy regardless of result
Routine HPV screening assumes the patient is asymptomatic
Smoking is a co-factor for cervical cancer progression
Friable, contact-bleeding cervix warrants direct visualisation
Recent negative HPV does not exclude pathology in a symptomatic patient
explanation.pearls: The biggest pitfall in cervical cancer diagnosis is over-reliance on a recent negative screen in a symptomatic patient. Symptoms override screening (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Advanced cervical cancer - recognising locally invasive symptoms
stem: A 52-year-old woman who has never participated in cervical screening attends ED with a 6-week history of intermittent vaginal bleeding (now soaking through several pads daily), foul-smelling watery discharge, deep right-sided pelvic and lower-back pain, new right leg swelling extending to the thigh, and frank haematuria for the past week. She has lost 6 kg unintentionally over 3 months and feels exhausted. She is a former heavy smoker (30 pack-years, quit 2 years ago), G3P3 with all vaginal deliveries, and has had three lifetime partners. She received no HPV vaccination. She has no significant past medical history. She emigrated to Australia from a country without an organised cervical screening program at age 30 and has had no screening since. She takes no regular medications and has no allergies. Bowels are regular and there is no rectal bleeding. She is otherwise afebrile.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/68 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 18, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 98%.
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae. Right leg pitting oedema to thigh, non-tender, no calf cord. Abdomen: tender suprapubic mass, no peritonism. Speculum: large exophytic friable mass replacing the cervix, contact bleeds easily, foul discharge. Bimanual: fixed irregular mass extending into right parametrium.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78 g/L (115-165), MCV 76 fL, ferritin 8 mcg/L. Urea 12 mmol/L, creatinine 168 micromol/L (baseline unknown). Urinalysis: blood 3+, protein 1+. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which combination of clinical features most strongly suggests locally advanced cervical cancer with parametrial / pelvic sidewall involvement?

options.text: Heavy menstrual bleeding with iron-deficiency anaemia and a bulky uterus
options.rationale: This pattern is non-specific and characterises benign causes (e.g. fibroid uterus) rather than locally advanced cervical cancer, which involves parametrial fixation and adjacent organ invasion (RANZCOG; Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Postcoital bleeding with a small, mobile cervix and normal renal function
options.rationale: Postcoital bleeding with a mobile cervix and normal renal function is more consistent with early-stage cervical disease or benign causes (e.g. cervical ectropion, polyp); it lacks features of local extension (RANZCOG).

options.text: Unilateral lower-limb oedema, haematuria, rising creatinine and a fixed parametrial mass
options.rationale: Unilateral leg oedema reflects iliac/pelvic-sidewall lymphatic or venous obstruction, haematuria with rising creatinine suggests bladder invasion or ureteric obstruction with hydronephrosis, and a fixed parametrial mass indicates extracervical spread. Together these are features of locally advanced cervical cancer (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Postmenopausal bleeding with an endometrial thickness of 5 mm on TVUS
options.rationale: Postmenopausal bleeding with a thickened endometrium is the syndrome of endometrial, not cervical, cancer; cervical cancer typically presents earlier and with cervix-based symptoms (RANZCOG).

options.text: Bilateral lower abdominal pain with adnexal tenderness, pyrexia and elevated WCC
options.rationale: This pattern fits pelvic inflammatory disease or tubo-ovarian abscess; locally advanced cervical cancer is typically afebrile unless there is a superimposed pyometra (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Locally advanced cervical cancer extends laterally into the parametrium (palpable as a fixed cervix), can occlude ureters (hydronephrosis, rising creatinine), invades bladder (haematuria) or rectum (rectal bleeding, fistula), and causes lymphatic and venous obstruction in the pelvic sidewall (unilateral leg oedema). Recognising these features prompts urgent gynae-oncology referral (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
key_points: Parametrial extension presents as a fixed cervix on bimanual examination
Ureteric obstruction -> hydronephrosis, rising creatinine, AKI
Bladder invasion -> haematuria; rectal invasion -> rectal bleeding/fistula
Pelvic sidewall lymphatic/venous obstruction -> unilateral leg oedema
Under-screened women (migrants, refugees, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander women) over-represented in advanced presentations
explanation.pearls: Cervical cancer remains a disease of inequitable access - 80% of Australian cervical cancers occur in under-screened or never-screened women (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Postmenopausal bleeding - TVUS endometrial thickness >4 mm threshold
stem: A 64-year-old G3P3 woman attends her GP with two episodes of fresh vaginal bleeding over the past 3 weeks. She experienced natural menopause at age 51 with no further bleeding until now. She has no postcoital bleeding, no discharge and no pelvic pain. Her last cervical screening test was 4 years ago, HPV not detected. She has type 2 diabetes (metformin 1 g BD, last HbA1c 7.4%), essential hypertension on perindopril 5 mg daily, BMI 34, and a 25-year history of regular menses prior to menopause. She has never used hormone therapy or tamoxifen. Family history is unremarkable for gynaecological malignancy and Lynch syndrome screening criteria are not met. She does not smoke. She walks 3 km daily and has no urinary or bowel red flags. She has had no recent intercourse and is not on anticoagulation. She is not anaemic on point-of-care testing. She is anxious because her neighbour was recently diagnosed with endometrial cancer.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.5 degC.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no masses. Speculum: atrophic vaginal mucosa, no cervical lesion, small amount of blood at the os, no friability. Bimanual: uterus normal size, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 124 g/L (115-165). Urinalysis negative. Transvaginal ultrasound: endometrium 8 mm with heterogeneous echotexture; no focal mass identified; both ovaries normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer for outpatient hysteroscopy with directed and Pipelle endometrial biopsy
options.rationale: Postmenopausal bleeding with endometrial thickness >4 mm on TVUS requires histological assessment; outpatient hysteroscopy with directed and Pipelle biopsy is the recommended pathway, with the higher diagnostic yield than blind Pipelle alone when the endometrium is thickened or heterogeneous (RANZCOG; eviQ Endometrial cancer).

options.text: Trial topical vaginal oestrogen for presumed atrophic vaginitis and review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Vaginal atrophy is a common cause of postmenopausal bleeding but cannot be assumed when the endometrium is >4 mm; histological exclusion of endometrial pathology is required first (RANZCOG).

options.text: Reassure as a single episode and review in 3 months if symptoms persist
options.rationale: Any postmenopausal bleeding warrants investigation; reassurance without histology is unsafe given an endometrial cancer prevalence of approximately 10% in postmenopausal bleeding (RANZCOG; eviQ).

options.text: Order a serum CA-125 to risk-stratify endometrial pathology before further imaging
options.rationale: CA-125 is used in ovarian cancer risk-stratification, not endometrial; histology directly addresses the diagnostic question and should not be delayed (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat the transvaginal ultrasound in 6 weeks to assess for any change
options.rationale: Repeat imaging would delay diagnosis without altering the indication for histology in a postmenopausal woman with bleeding and endometrium >4 mm (eviQ Endometrial cancer).

explanation.summary: Any episode of postmenopausal bleeding must be investigated. TVUS endometrial thickness of >4 mm in a postmenopausal woman not on HRT, or any focal endometrial abnormality, mandates histological assessment via hysteroscopy and biopsy (RANZCOG; eviQ).
key_points: Any PMB requires investigation - approximately 10% have endometrial cancer
TVUS endometrial thickness threshold for biopsy: >4 mm (postmenopausal, no HRT)
Persistent or recurrent PMB requires biopsy even if endometrium <4 mm
Hysteroscopy + directed biopsy outperforms blind Pipelle when endometrium is thickened/focal
Risk factors: obesity, T2DM, unopposed oestrogen, nulliparity, late menopause, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome
explanation.pearls: If a postmenopausal woman has bleeding but endometrium is <=4 mm with otherwise reassuring TVUS, reassure but warn that recurrent bleeding mandates histology regardless of thickness (RANZCOG).

subtopic_detail: Endometrial hyperplasia with atypia - management in postmenopausal woman
stem: A 58-year-old G2P2 postmenopausal woman is referred to gynaecology after 4 weeks of intermittent vaginal bleeding. TVUS demonstrated an endometrial thickness of 12 mm with a heterogeneous appearance and no focal mass. Outpatient hysteroscopy with directed and Pipelle biopsies reports endometrial hyperplasia with atypia (also called endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia, EIN); no invasive disease seen on the available tissue. She has type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.6%, metformin 1 g BD), hypertension treated with perindopril 5 mg daily, BMI 38, and natural menopause at age 53. She has no family history of Lynch-associated cancers. She has completed her family. She has no contraindications to surgery, ASA 2, and is keen to 'sort this out properly'. She does not wish to continue hormonal therapy long term. She has no thromboembolic history and a normal cardiac assessment on preoperative review. She asks specifically about the risk that this finding already represents cancer.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, no masses. Bimanual: uterus normal size, mobile, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Pipelle + hysteroscopic directed biopsy: endometrial hyperplasia with atypia (EIN). Hb 122 g/L. TVUS: ET 12 mm heterogeneous, both ovaries normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate definitive management?

options.text: Total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy as definitive surgical management
options.rationale: Atypical endometrial hyperplasia (EIN) carries a concurrent endometrial carcinoma rate of approximately 30-40% on final hysterectomy specimens and a substantial future progression risk; in a postmenopausal woman who has completed her family, total hysterectomy with BSO is the recommended definitive treatment (RANZCOG; eviQ Endometrial cancer; RCOG/BSGE Endometrial hyperplasia).

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system with 6-monthly endometrial sampling for surveillance
options.rationale: LNG-IUS with surveillance is reserved for premenopausal patients who wish to retain fertility or for those unfit for surgery; in a postmenopausal woman who has completed her family, definitive surgery is preferred given the high concurrent cancer rate (RCOG/BSGE Endometrial hyperplasia).

options.text: Oral medroxyprogesterone acetate with repeat biopsy in 12 months
options.rationale: Oral progestogens are less effective than LNG-IUS for hyperplasia regression and would not be first-line for atypical hyperplasia in a surgical candidate who has completed her family (RCOG/BSGE Endometrial hyperplasia).

options.text: Endometrial ablation to control bleeding and reduce cancer risk
options.rationale: Endometrial ablation is contraindicated where there is hyperplasia with atypia or suspected malignancy because residual buried endometrium can harbour or develop cancer that is then difficult to detect (RANZCOG; RCOG/BSGE Endometrial hyperplasia).

options.text: Watchful waiting with repeat TVUS and endometrial biopsy in 6 months
options.rationale: Watchful waiting is not safe for atypical hyperplasia given the high concurrent and progression risk to invasive cancer; treatment is required (eviQ Endometrial cancer).

explanation.summary: Endometrial hyperplasia with atypia (EIN) carries a 30-40% concurrent endometrial cancer rate. Total hysterectomy with BSO is the standard of care in postmenopausal women or those who have completed their family. Fertility-sparing LNG-IUS is reserved for younger patients or those unfit for surgery (RCOG/BSGE).
key_points: Hyperplasia WITHOUT atypia: LNG-IUS first-line, repeat biopsy at 6 months
Hyperplasia WITH atypia (EIN): hysterectomy + BSO if postmenopausal or completed family
Fertility-sparing option in atypia: LNG-IUS with 3-monthly biopsy until regression
Endometrial ablation contraindicated when atypia/malignancy is present or suspected
Risk factors: obesity, T2DM, unopposed oestrogen, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome
explanation.pearls: Even with formal hysteroscopy plus Pipelle, 30-40% of atypical hyperplasia cases harbour an undetected concurrent endometrial cancer at hysterectomy (eviQ Endometrial cancer).

subtopic_detail: Ovarian cancer recognition - vague abdominal symptoms + CA-125
stem: A 62-year-old G2P2 postmenopausal woman attends her GP with a 3-month history of progressive abdominal bloating, early satiety, mild lower abdominal discomfort, increased urinary frequency without dysuria, and unintentional 4 kg weight loss. She initially thought she had developed IBS. There is no rectal bleeding, no change in bowel habit beyond mild constipation, and no postmenopausal bleeding. She has no significant past medical history, no family history of ovarian, breast or bowel cancer, and is not known to carry BRCA1/2. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol minimally. She has had no abdominal surgery. Her last cervical screening test was 2 years ago, HPV not detected. She has not used hormone therapy. Examination reveals mild abdominal distension and a vaguely palpable left adnexal fullness. There is no overt ascites but a low-volume effect cannot be excluded clinically. She is afebrile, with stable observations. The GP has already started her on a fibre supplement which has not relieved symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen mildly distended. Vague left lower-quadrant fullness, no peritonism. Bimanual: left adnexal mass approximately 7 cm, partly fixed; uterus normal size.
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal. Urea, creatinine, LFTs within normal limits. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Colonoscopy as the initial investigation for presumed bowel pathology and constipation
options.rationale: While colorectal cancer is on the differential for these symptoms, a palpable adnexal mass in a postmenopausal woman shifts priority to gynaecological assessment; bowel investigation may follow, but is not the first step (RANZCOG; Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).

options.text: Serum HE4 alone as a more specific tumour marker than CA-125
options.rationale: HE4 is not the established Australian first-line marker; CA-125 is incorporated in RMI and the Australian pathway, with HE4 used in some ROMA-based pathways internationally (Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).

options.text: Trial of antispasmodic and follow up if symptoms persist beyond 6 weeks
options.rationale: Trialling antispasmodics in a postmenopausal woman with a palpable adnexal mass risks delaying ovarian cancer diagnosis; symptoms persistent beyond 4 weeks already warrant urgent investigation (Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).

options.text: CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast as first-line imaging investigation
options.rationale: CT has a role in staging once malignancy is suspected, but pelvic ultrasound is the recommended first-line imaging for an adnexal mass because it characterises the lesion (solid components, septations, vascularity) to support RMI/IOTA scoring (Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).

options.text: Pelvic transvaginal and transabdominal ultrasound with serum CA-125 for RMI/IOTA risk stratification
options.rationale: Persistent abdominal bloating, early satiety, urinary frequency or pelvic pain in a postmenopausal woman, particularly with a palpable adnexal mass, requires urgent gynaecological workup. The Australian pathway is pelvic ultrasound (transvaginal + transabdominal) plus serum CA-125; together these enable Risk of Malignancy Index (RMI) or IOTA-based triage to a gynaecological oncology MDT (RANZCOG; Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).

explanation.summary: Ovarian cancer typically presents with vague abdominal symptoms (bloating, early satiety, urinary frequency, abdominal/pelvic pain) lasting more than a few weeks; in postmenopausal women these symptoms must trigger TVUS plus CA-125. The combination feeds RMI/IOTA scoring and triages referral to a gynaecological oncology MDT (Cancer Australia; RANZCOG).
key_points: Symptoms: persistent bloating, early satiety, urinary urgency/frequency, pelvic/abdominal pain
First-line investigation: TVUS + transabdominal US + serum CA-125
Risk of Malignancy Index (RMI) = U x M x CA-125, with US features, menopausal status
IOTA simple rules / ADNEX model for ultrasound-based malignancy risk
All suspicious adnexal masses in postmenopausal women -> gynae oncology MDT
explanation.pearls: Ovarian cancer is often labelled the 'silent killer' but a careful history elicits 3-6 months of persistent symptoms in most patients; the key skill is taking these complaints seriously in postmenopausal women (Cancer Australia ovarian cancer).

subtopic_detail: Ovarian torsion - emergency recognition and detorsion
stem: A 24-year-old G0 woman presents to ED at 02:00 with sudden-onset severe right iliac fossa pain that woke her from sleep 4 hours ago, associated with nausea and two episodes of vomiting. The pain is colicky, radiates to the right groin and posterior thigh, and is currently 9/10 in intensity despite paracetamol 1 g PO taken 90 minutes ago. She had a recent pelvic ultrasound 2 weeks ago for cyclical pelvic discomfort showing a 6 cm right ovarian dermoid (mature cystic teratoma); she was awaiting an outpatient gynaecology appointment. Her last menstrual period was 10 days ago, regular 28-day cycles, and she has no intermenstrual bleeding or discharge. She is sexually active in a long-term relationship, uses condoms, and beta-hCG taken on arrival is negative. She has no fevers, no urinary symptoms and no diarrhoea or rectal bleeding. Her partner reports she has not eaten or drunk anything since the pain began. She has no relevant past medical history, no allergies, and takes only ibuprofen as required for cyclical pain. On examination she is in obvious distress, splinted on the right, with cool peripheries from pain. She has not previously had any abdominal or pelvic surgery and has never been pregnant.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, HR 112 bpm, RR 22, T 37.1 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Abdomen: marked right iliac fossa tenderness with voluntary guarding, no peritonism. Bowel sounds present. Bimanual: very tender right adnexal mass, palpable approximately 7 cm, with severe pain on cervical motion. Uterus normal size, anteverted.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative. WCC 13.2 x10^9/L, CRP 12 mg/L. Urinalysis negative. Bedside TVUS: enlarged right ovary 9 cm with peripheral follicles and a 6 cm complex mass; minimal Doppler flow within the ovarian parenchyma compared with the left.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Admit for serial observation, IV analgesia and a formal radiology TVUS at 08:00
options.rationale: Delaying definitive assessment risks complete vascular compromise and ovarian loss; torsion is a time-critical diagnosis and surgical exploration should not wait for elective imaging (RANZCOG; RCH CPG: Ovarian torsion).

options.text: Emergency CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast before any surgical decision
options.rationale: Ultrasound is the imaging of choice; CT adds delay and radiation without changing surgical management when ultrasound already shows an enlarged ovary with reduced Doppler flow and a known dermoid (RANZCOG).

options.text: Right salpingo-oophorectomy via laparotomy because the ovary is likely non-viable
options.rationale: Even ovaries appearing dusky or black at surgery should be detorted and reassessed because parenchyma often recovers; oophorectomy as initial action sacrifices fertility unnecessarily (RANZCOG; RCH CPG: Ovarian torsion).

options.text: Urgent diagnostic laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian-preserving cystectomy under general anaesthetic
options.rationale: Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, an enlarged ovary with peripheral follicles and reduced Doppler flow on TVUS, and a known ovarian mass strongly suggest ovarian torsion; emergency laparoscopy with detorsion and conservative cystectomy preserves ovarian function. Doppler flow is unreliable for excluding torsion and clinical suspicion should drive surgery (RANZCOG; RCH CPG: Ovarian torsion).

options.text: IV opioid analgesia and discharge with outpatient gynaecology follow-up next week
options.rationale: Discharging a patient with suspected torsion is unsafe; emergency surgical management is the standard of care (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion is a time-critical surgical emergency. Suspicion is clinical and ultrasound supports the diagnosis (enlarged ovary, peripheral follicles, reduced/absent Doppler flow), but preserved Doppler does not exclude torsion. Management is emergency laparoscopy, detorsion and conservative cystectomy where possible to preserve fertility (RANZCOG; RCH CPG).
key_points: Risk factors: ovarian cyst >5 cm, dermoid, pregnancy, ovulation induction
Hallmarks: sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, nausea/vomiting
TVUS: enlarged ovary, peripheral follicles, abnormal Doppler (but preserved flow doesn't exclude)
Treat with emergency laparoscopy + detorsion + cystectomy
Sacrifice ovary only if necrotic and not recoverable after detorsion period
explanation.pearls: Doppler is sensitive but not specific - up to 60% of confirmed torsions have preserved Doppler flow at the time of imaging. Clinical suspicion takes precedence (RANZCOG).

subtopic_detail: Lichen sclerosus - ultrapotent topical steroid + cancer surveillance
stem: A 68-year-old postmenopausal woman attends a vulval clinic with a 12-month history of progressive vulval pruritus, soreness, and dyspareunia limiting intercourse with her long-term partner. She has noticed white, paper-thin skin around the labia minora and perineum, with occasional fissuring and contact bleeding when itching at night and during attempts at intercourse. Multiple courses of clotrimazole pessaries and over-the-counter hydrocortisone 1% over the past 6 months have not helped. She has had no postmenopausal bleeding from the cervix and her last cervical screening test 12 months ago was HPV not detected. She has Hashimoto's thyroiditis on stable thyroxine 75 mcg daily, well-controlled hypertension on perindopril 5 mg, BMI 28, and no immunosuppression. She has not used local or systemic oestrogen replacement. She has no personal or family history of vulval, cervical, breast or other gynaecological cancer. On examination there is well-demarcated 'figure-of-eight' white atrophic change involving the labia minora, clitoral hood and perianal skin, with partial resorption of the labia minora, narrowing of the introitus, and early posterior fourchette adhesion. There is one persistent indurated nodule 8 mm in diameter on the right labium majus that has been present for 6 weeks and has not responded to topical hydrocortisone. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy.

data_table.Examination: Atrophic, paper-thin white change in classic figure-of-eight distribution. Loss of labia minora architecture. Discrete 8 mm indurated nodule on right labium majus, firm, non-tender. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening 12 months ago: HPV not detected.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Punch biopsy the indurated nodule and start clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment nightly
options.rationale: The clinical picture is classic for lichen sclerosus; treatment is ultrapotent topical steroid (clobetasol propionate 0.05%) typically nightly for 4 weeks, alternate nights for 4 weeks, then twice weekly maintenance. The 4-5% lifetime risk of vulval SCC means any persistent indurated, ulcerated or pigmented lesion must be biopsied promptly (RANZCOG; BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

options.text: Trial topical oestrogen cream for presumed atrophic vaginitis and review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: The findings (figure-of-eight white atrophic change, architectural loss) are classic for lichen sclerosus, not generalised atrophic vaginitis; topical oestrogen would not address the diagnosis or the suspicious nodule (RANZCOG).

options.text: Commence clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment nightly and review again in 12 weeks
options.rationale: Although ultrapotent topical steroids are the mainstay treatment for lichen sclerosus, the new indurated nodule must be biopsied first because lichen sclerosus carries a 4-5% lifetime risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma, and an indurated persistent lesion is suspicious (RANZCOG; BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

options.text: Refer for wide local excision of the entire affected vulval area
options.rationale: Vulvectomy or wide excision is not the standard treatment for lichen sclerosus; medical control with ultrapotent steroids is first-line and surgery is reserved for biopsy-proven malignancy or symptomatic architectural distortion (BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

options.text: Refer to dermatology for systemic methotrexate as steroid-sparing therapy now
options.rationale: Systemic immunosuppression is reserved for refractory disease unresponsive to ultrapotent topical steroids; it is not first-line, and the immediate clinical priority is biopsy of the suspicious nodule (BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

explanation.summary: Lichen sclerosus is treated with ultrapotent topical corticosteroid (clobetasol propionate 0.05%) in a tapering regimen with maintenance. Vulval SCC develops in 4-5% lifetime, so any persistent indurated, ulcerated, pigmented or non-responding lesion must be biopsied. Lifelong surveillance is required (RANZCOG; BAD).
key_points: Classic appearance: figure-of-eight white atrophic vulval skin with architectural loss
First-line: clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment - induction then taper to maintenance
Lifetime vulval SCC risk: approximately 4-5%
Biopsy any persistent indurated, ulcerated, pigmented, or steroid-unresponsive lesion
Lifelong surveillance review at least annually
explanation.pearls: Patient self-application is a key skill; demonstrate with a mirror, advise a fingertip-unit per application, and emphasise that maintenance therapy reduces both symptoms and the risk of malignant progression (BAD lichen sclerosus guideline).

subtopic_detail: Bartholin abscess - Word catheter as primary care management
stem: A 28-year-old G1P1 woman presents to her GP with a 3-day history of progressively painful right-sided labial swelling. The swelling is now egg-sized, tense, exquisitely tender and is interfering with sitting and walking. She has had a low-grade fever of 37.8 degC at home but has not had rigors. She has had no abnormal vaginal discharge for the past 3 months, no postcoital or intermenstrual bleeding, no urinary symptoms and no constitutional symptoms. She is in a monogamous relationship of 5 years and her most recent STI screen 4 months ago was negative for chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis and HIV. She had a similar but smaller episode on the same side 2 years ago that resolved with incision and drainage in ED but recurred 3 months later as a painless cyst that has since fluctuated in size. She has no allergies, takes no regular medications, is not pregnant (LMP 10 days ago, regular cycles), and has no other significant medical history. The GP clinic has Word catheters in stock, the GP is trained in their insertion under local anaesthetic, and the patient consents to a procedure today after written information.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/76 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 37.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Tense, fluctuant 4 cm swelling at the right posterior introitus (5 o'clock position), overlying erythema, exquisitely tender. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. No vulval ulceration. Speculum deferred due to pain.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management of this Bartholin abscess?

options.text: Simple incision and drainage with packing, no catheter or marsupialisation
options.rationale: Simple I&D without a Word catheter or marsupialisation has a high recurrence rate (up to 40%); the duct often re-closes once the cavity collapses, particularly in a patient with prior recurrence (RANZCOG; KEMH Bartholin abscess).

options.text: Outpatient oral antibiotics alone (e.g. amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BD) without drainage
options.rationale: Antibiotic monotherapy without drainage rarely resolves a tense fluctuant abscess; drainage is the primary intervention, with antibiotics reserved for systemic features, cellulitis, immunosuppression or pregnancy (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

options.text: Incision and drainage with Word catheter insertion retained in situ 4-6 weeks
options.rationale: Word catheter placement after incision and drainage is the recommended primary-care option for an acute Bartholin abscess: it maintains an epithelialised tract while the cavity heals, with a recurrence rate substantially lower than simple I&D. The catheter is removed after 4-6 weeks (RANZCOG; KEMH Bartholin abscess).

options.text: Excision of the Bartholin gland under general anaesthetic as definitive management
options.rationale: Excision is reserved for recurrent abscesses unresponsive to drainage with Word catheter or marsupialisation, or for suspected Bartholin gland carcinoma (especially in women over 40 with a solid mass); it carries higher morbidity (bleeding, dyspareunia) than less invasive options (RANZCOG).

options.text: Sitz baths and oral analgesia, with re-review in 1 week if not improving
options.rationale: Conservative measures are insufficient for a tense fluctuant abscess; while sitz baths help drainage of small early lesions, definitive drainage is required for an established abscess of this size (KEMH Bartholin abscess).

explanation.summary: An acute Bartholin abscess is best managed with incision and drainage plus Word catheter placement to maintain an epithelialised tract for 4-6 weeks. Marsupialisation is an effective alternative, particularly for recurrence. Simple I&D alone has a recurrence rate up to 40%. Antibiotics are reserved for systemic features, cellulitis, immunosuppression or pregnancy (RANZCOG; KEMH).
key_points: First-line: I&D + Word catheter, retained 4-6 weeks
Alternative: marsupialisation (suturing cyst wall to skin) - durable but requires more skill
Simple I&D alone -> high recurrence rate
Antibiotics indicated for cellulitis, sepsis, immunosuppression, pregnancy
Excision considered for refractory recurrence or suspected Bartholin gland carcinoma (esp. age >40 with solid mass)
explanation.pearls: In women over 40 with a persistent solid Bartholin mass rather than cystic abscess, biopsy/excision is essential to exclude Bartholin gland carcinoma (RANZCOG).

subtopic_detail: Test of cure after LLETZ for HSIL
stem: A 34-year-old G0 woman had a LLETZ procedure 11 months ago for biopsy-proven HSIL (CIN3) detected after a screening HPV test reported HPV 16 with possible HSIL on reflex cytology. The LLETZ histology confirmed CIN3 with clear excision margins (ectocervical, endocervical and deep margins all clear of dysplasia, no glandular involvement, no microinvasion). Post-LLETZ recovery was uncomplicated with no secondary haemorrhage and no cervical stenosis on subsequent review. She is asymptomatic, with no postcoital or intermenstrual bleeding and no discharge. She is in a monogamous relationship of 2 years and is using the etonogestrel implant for contraception. She is a non-smoker, BMI 24, has no immunosuppression, takes no regular medications and has no family history of cervical or other gynaecological malignancy. She received Gardasil as a teenager and completed her course. She attends her GP today asking what surveillance she now needs and when she can return to routine 5-yearly NCSP screening.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft. Cervix at speculum: smooth, no obvious lesion, no contact bleeding.

lead_in: Which surveillance pathway should she follow under the Australian NCSP?

options.text: Annual HPV cervical screening for 5 years, then return to routine 5-yearly screening
options.rationale: Annual HPV testing is not the NCSP test-of-cure pathway; the structured test of cure uses HPV with reflex LBC (co-test) at 12 months then yearly until two consecutive negative co-tests are obtained (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Single co-test at 24 months and return to routine 5-yearly screening if negative
options.rationale: A single co-test is not adequate; two consecutive negative co-tests at 12 and 24 months are required before returning to routine screening (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Return to routine 5-yearly NCSP screening now because excision margins were clear
options.rationale: Clear excision margins reduce but do not eliminate the risk of recurrent HSIL; structured surveillance is required because residual HPV is the strongest predictor of recurrent disease (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: 6-monthly colposcopy for 2 years regardless of cytology or HPV result
options.rationale: Routine 6-monthly colposcopy is not the NCSP pathway after a successfully treated CIN3 with clear margins; cytological/HPV co-test surveillance is sufficient unless test of cure detects abnormality (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

options.text: Co-test at 12 and 24 months; both negative returns to 5-yearly screening
options.rationale: After treatment of HSIL (CIN2/3), the NCSP test-of-cure protocol is a co-test (HPV + LBC on the same sample) at 12 and 24 months post-treatment. If both are HPV not detected and LBC negative, the patient returns to routine 5-yearly HPV screening. Any positive result triggers colposcopy referral (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines; NCSP National Cancer Screening Register).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

explanation.summary: After treatment of CIN2/3 (HSIL), the NCSP test-of-cure pathway is a co-test (HPV + LBC) at 12 and 24 months. Two consecutive negative co-tests return the patient to routine 5-yearly screening. Any positive result triggers colposcopy referral. Persistent HPV positivity is the strongest predictor of recurrent disease (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).
key_points: Test of cure after HSIL treatment: co-test at 12 and 24 months
Two consecutive negative co-tests -> return to routine 5-yearly NCSP screening
Any positive at test of cure -> colposcopy referral
Adenocarcinoma in situ (AIS) treatment requires longer surveillance
Immunosuppressed patients have a more intensive surveillance protocol
explanation.pearls: Persistent HPV is the strongest single predictor of recurrent CIN2/3 after LLETZ - more important than margin status alone (Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines).

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia NCSP Clinical Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Tamoxifen-related endometrial bleeding - investigate symptoms, do not screen asymptomatic
stem: A 64-year-old G2P2 woman has been taking tamoxifen 20 mg daily for 4 years as adjuvant endocrine therapy for ER-positive, PR-positive, HER2-negative, node-negative left breast cancer treated 4 years ago with breast-conserving surgery and adjuvant radiotherapy. She was previously postmenopausal (natural menopause age 52, no bleeding for 12 years prior to commencing tamoxifen) and is now referred to gynaecology after presenting to her GP with two episodes of fresh vaginal bleeding over the past 3 weeks, each lasting 2-3 days and requiring two pads daily. She has had no postcoital bleeding, no discharge, no pelvic pain, no abdominal bloating and no urinary or bowel symptoms. Her last cervical screening 18 months ago was HPV not detected on a clinician-collected sample. She has hypertension (perindopril 5 mg daily, BP 138/82 on review), a BMI of 31, and well-controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 6.9%, metformin 1 g BD). She has no personal or family history of Lynch-associated cancers and meets no Lynch testing criteria. She has never used hormone therapy or unopposed oestrogen pre-tamoxifen. She takes no anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents and has no history of VTE. She is fully adherent to tamoxifen, has not missed doses, and is keen to complete her planned 10-year adjuvant course but is anxious that bleeding may signal cancer. Her breast oncologist's letter notes a low risk of breast cancer recurrence on validated risk calculators but does not advise on gynaecological management. She has had no abnormal surveillance mammograms.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft. Speculum: atrophic mucosa, small amount of blood at the os, no cervical lesion. Bimanual: uterus normal size, mobile, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 124 g/L (115-165). TVUS performed by GP: endometrium 9 mm, heterogeneous, with several small cystic spaces; no focal mass identified; ovaries normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discontinue tamoxifen and substitute an aromatase inhibitor without further gynaecological investigation
options.rationale: Stopping tamoxifen unilaterally compromises breast cancer outcomes; substitution decisions belong to the breast MDT after gynaecological diagnosis is established. Tamoxifen change does not address the immediate need to exclude endometrial pathology (eviQ Endometrial cancer; eviQ Tamoxifen).

options.text: Refer for outpatient hysteroscopy with directed and Pipelle endometrial biopsy
options.rationale: Postmenopausal bleeding on tamoxifen requires structural and histological assessment. Tamoxifen causes subepithelial stromal change and cystic atrophy that thicken the endometrium on TVUS even in benign disease, making the standard >4 mm threshold less reliable; hysteroscopy with directed biopsy is the recommended investigation in any symptomatic tamoxifen user. Routine TVUS surveillance of asymptomatic tamoxifen users is NOT recommended in Australian guidance (RANZCOG; eviQ Endometrial cancer; eviQ Tamoxifen).

options.text: Repeat TVUS in 3 months to assess for any change in endometrial thickness
options.rationale: Repeat imaging delays diagnosis without changing the indication for histology in a symptomatic tamoxifen user; TVUS findings in this population are also poorly specific for malignancy due to tamoxifen-related cystic stromal change (eviQ Endometrial cancer).

options.text: Reassure as endometrial thickness is acceptable for tamoxifen users; review if bleeding recurs
options.rationale: The standard >4 mm threshold cannot be applied to symptomatic tamoxifen users, and any postmenopausal bleeding on tamoxifen requires histological exclusion of cancer; reassurance is unsafe (eviQ Tamoxifen).

options.text: Commence routine 6-monthly TVUS surveillance and biopsy only if thickness exceeds 8 mm
options.rationale: Asymptomatic surveillance of tamoxifen users with TVUS is NOT recommended in Australian guidance; surveillance does not improve outcomes and frequently triggers unnecessary investigations. Bleeding, not thickness, is the investigation trigger in tamoxifen users (eviQ Tamoxifen; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Tamoxifen approximately doubles endometrial cancer risk in postmenopausal users. Routine TVUS surveillance is NOT recommended for asymptomatic users because tamoxifen-related stromal change reduces the specificity of endometrial thickness measurements. Any abnormal bleeding requires hysteroscopy with directed biopsy. Treatment decisions about continuing tamoxifen are made by the breast MDT (eviQ Tamoxifen; eviQ Endometrial cancer; RANZCOG).
key_points: Tamoxifen has anti-oestrogenic effects on breast but pro-oestrogenic on endometrium
Approximately 2-fold increase in endometrial cancer risk in postmenopausal users
No asymptomatic TVUS surveillance - it doesn't improve outcomes
Any abnormal bleeding -> hysteroscopy + directed biopsy
TVUS thickness threshold of >4 mm is not reliable in tamoxifen users
Tamoxifen continuation decisions made by breast MDT after gynae diagnosis
explanation.pearls: The 'investigate symptoms, don't screen asymptomatic' principle is paramount for tamoxifen surveillance - it is one of the few oncology contexts where active surveillance is explicitly NOT recommended (eviQ Tamoxifen).
